name: Will-o-the-Wisp
description: Will-o-the-Wisps, also known as ignis fatuus ('foolish fire'), are mysterious lights often seen flickering over bogs, swamps, and marshes. They are described as ethereal, dancing flames that appear to recede when approached, leading travelers astray. Folklore suggests they are mischievous spirits, souls of the dead, or fairies attempting to lure people to their doom, often into dangerous terrain. Some believe they are guardians of treasure or markers of hidden pathways, while others see them as omens of misfortune. Their appearance has been attributed to various natural phenomena, such as the combustion of gases from decaying organic matter, but their enduring presence in folklore suggests a deeper, more symbolic meaning related to the unknown and the dangers of the natural world. Tales of Will-o-the-Wisps serve as cautionary stories, warning against the perils of straying from established paths and trusting appearances, especially in wild and untamed landscapes. The allure of the lights represents the temptation to follow the unknown, with potentially disastrous consequences. They embody the mystery and danger inherent in nature, capturing the imagination and serving as a reminder of the power of folklore to explain the unexplained.
subtitle: Mischievous lights leading travelers astray
appearance: Will-o-the-Wisps typically appear as small, flickering lights, often described as blue, green, or yellow in color. They can range in size from a small candle flame to a larger, more substantial glow. They often dance and move erratically, making them appear almost alive. Some accounts describe them as having a faint, almost human-like shape within the light, while others simply see them as amorphous orbs of light.
habitat: Will-o-the-Wisps are most commonly found in marshes, bogs, swamps, and other damp, desolate areas. They are often associated with places where the boundary between the natural and supernatural worlds is perceived to be thin.
behavior: Will-o-the-Wisps are generally depicted as mischievous and deceptive. They lure travelers away from safe paths, leading them into dangerous or impassable terrain. Their behavior is unpredictable, sometimes appearing helpful before suddenly disappearing or leading people into traps. Some stories suggest they are malevolent, actively seeking to harm those who follow them, while others portray them as simply playful or indifferent to the consequences of their actions.

keyMyths.title: The Treacherous Light
keyMyths.description: A traveler, lost in the woods, follows a Will-o-the-Wisp, believing it will lead him to safety. Instead, it leads him deeper into the swamp, where he becomes hopelessly lost and never seen again.
keyMyths.source: Folk Tales of the English Countryside

sources.author: Katherine Briggs
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of fairy lore, including Will-o-the-Wisps.
sources.text: Comprehensive overview of fairy lore, including Will-o-the-Wisps.
